none: Cotton dust occupational exposure standard : environmental impact statement.

The U.S. Department of Labor's Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) proposed a new standard to limit workers' exposure to cotton dust in the textile industry. This was in response to the high incidence of respiratory diseases, such as byssinosis, among workers. The proposed standard aimed to reduce permissible exposure limits and improve workplace conditions through better dust control measures. The initiative was expected to improve the health of workers without significantly impacting the environment outside the workplace.
Cotton dust occupational exposure standard : environmental impact statement.

The U.S. Department of Labor's Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) proposed a new standard to limit workers' exposure to cotton dust in the textile industry. This was in response to the high incidence of respiratory diseases, such as byssinosis, among workers. The proposed standard aimed to reduce permissible exposure limits and improve workplace conditions through better dust control measures. The initiative was expected to improve the health of workers without significantly impacting the environment outside the workplace.
United States, with specific mentions of Texas, California, Mississippi, and Arkansas
North Carolina Public Interest Research Group

The proposal for a new occupational exposure standard for cotton dust was driven by alarming rates of respiratory illnesses among textile workers, particularly in states like Texas, California, Mississippi, and Arkansas. There was some public concern regarding the potential economic impact on the textile industry, but the health benefits for workers were prioritized. Ultimately, the standard aimed to enhance worker safety and health without major environmental repercussions.
"The symptoms and physiological changes can be established by a non-physician equally as well as they can be established by a physician." — S.A. Roach, Imperial Chemical Industries
"The feelings among affected industries in West Texas is that they will probably never be able to meet the new standard." — U.S. Department of Labor, Lubbock, Texas
